title: NMS Thresholds
concept: NMS relies on two thresholds: a confidence threshold to discard weak boxes, and an IoU threshold to suppress duplicate overlaps.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is the main action of NMS?

options.text: To keep the highest confidence box and suppress overlapping duplicates
To calculate the coordinates of anchor boxes

exercises.explain: NMS cleans up duplicate boxes by suppressing overlaps relative to a high-confidence target.

cards.title: Too Strict vs. Too Loose
cards.body: If you are grading homework, setting a threshold of 99% is too strict — almost everyone fails. Setting a threshold of 10% is too loose — everyone passes, even if they wrote nonsense. Choosing NMS thresholds is a similar balancing act.

cards.title: NMS Threshold Parameters
cards.body: NMS uses two main thresholds: 1) Confidence Threshold (e.g. 0.3) to immediately discard weak predictions, and 2) IoU Threshold (e.g. 0.5) to define how much overlap is allowed before a box is suppressed.

cards.title: The Concert Entry
cards.body: Think of the confidence threshold as the ticket price: you must pay a minimum (e.g., 30% confidence) to enter. Think of the IoU threshold as the seating rules: if two people try to sit in the same seat (high overlap), only the one with the better ticket gets to stay.

cards.title: Adjusting the IoU Threshold
cards.body: If you set the IoU threshold to 0.8 (very high), you allow boxes to overlap significantly without suppression. If you set it to 0.2 (very low), even slight overlaps will cause nearby boxes to get deleted.

cards.title: No single perfect value
cards.body: It's a myth that there is one perfect threshold set for all datasets. If objects are naturally crowded (like a bag of apples), you need a higher IoU threshold so the model doesn't suppress real, overlapping apples.

cards.title: IoU Threshold Impact
cards.body: Comparing different NMS IoU suppression thresholds:

right.text:
Gentle suppression.
Allows nearby objects to coexist.

exercises.prompt: What happens if you set the NMS IoU threshold too low (e.g., 0.1)?
exercises.explain: A low IoU threshold means even a tiny overlap triggers suppression, causing real, adjacent objects to be mistakenly deleted.

options.text: The model will suppress (delete) legitimate nearby objects because of minor overlaps
The model will stop classifying objects entirely
The model will output millions of duplicate boxes

exercises.prompt: True or False: The confidence threshold is applied AFTER NMS suppresses overlapping boxes.
exercises.explain: False. We apply the confidence threshold FIRST to filter out weak background noise boxes, leaving only strong candidates for NMS.
Increasing the IoU threshold allows crowded objects to overlap more without suppressing each other.
exercises.sentence: To prevent suppressing distinct objects in a crowded scene, we should ___ the IoU threshold.

exercises.prompt: Which configuration represents a standard post-processing filter combination for a detector?
exercises.explain: Confidence and IoU thresholds are the twin parameters configured for NMS post-processing.

exercises.prompt: You are building an object detector for a flock of birds flying closely together. The birds frequently overlap in the camera view. If your default NMS suppresses real birds, what adjustment should you make?

options.text: Increase the IoU threshold (e.g. from 0.45 to 0.70) to allow more overlap before boxes are suppressed.
Decrease the IoU threshold to 0.1 to clean up more boxes.

exercises.explain: Exactly! Raising the IoU threshold allows the model to keep boxes that overlap tightly, which is common in crowded scenes like flocks. Next up: let's learn how classical computer vision found candidate regions before neural networks!
